old_value: {"id":"bd-e83e","title":"Fix JoinError mapping in hdf5_writer.rs to use DaqError::TaskJoin","description":"PR #360 review: Two spawn_blocking sites in hdf5_writer.rs (lines ~219 and ~551) map JoinError to DaqError::Tokio(std::io::Error::new(...)), which fabricates an I/O error. Since DaqError has a dedicated TaskJoin(#[from] JoinError) variant, these should use .await?? or .map_err(DaqError::from)? to preserve panic/cancellation semantics. Both Copilot and CodeRabbit flagged this.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:37Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:37Z"}

{"id":"bd-9ts0","title":"Fix JoinError mapping in ring_buffer.rs to use DaqError::TaskJoin","description":"PR #360 review: Three spawn_blocking sites in ring_buffer.rs (read_snapshot, write, write_arrow_batch) map JoinError to DaqError::Storage(StorageError::new(StorageErrorKind::RingBuffer, ...)). Async boundary failures should map to DaqError::TaskJoin to keep them distinct from ring-buffer I/O errors. Replace the map_err closures with .await?? to let the From impl convert JoinError to TaskJoin.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:41Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:41Z"}

{"id":"bd-w5p3","title":"Fix StorageError partial move in error_mapping.rs","description":"PR #360 review: In map_daq_error_to_status, the arm DaqError::Storage(e) =\u003e match e.kind { ... } moves e.kind (StorageErrorKind is not Copy) and then uses e.message in each arm, causing a partial move compile error. Fix by matching on \u0026e.kind or destructuring StorageError { kind, message }.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:59Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:59Z"}

{"id":"bd-u1xw","title":"Validate set_poll_interval rejects Duration::ZERO","description":"PR #360 review: HDF5Writer::set_poll_interval accepts Duration::ZERO, which will panic when passed to tokio::time::interval(). Change the method to return Result\u003c()\u003e and validate that interval.is_zero() == false, returning a DaqError::Configuration error. Update all callers to handle the Result.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:56Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:56Z"}

{"id":"bd-kjxw","title":"Add DeadlineGuard for Rhai timeout panic safety","description":"PR #360 review: In execute_script_with_timeout (rhai_engine.rs), the deadline AtomicU64 is set before execute_script and cleared after. If execute_script panics or the task is cancelled, the deadline remains set, causing spurious timeouts on subsequent calls. Fix: introduce a Drop-based DeadlineGuard struct that stores the Arc\u003cAtomicU64\u003e and resets it to 0 in its Drop impl.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:45Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:45Z"}

{"id":"bd-nauq","title":"Switch Rhai timeout from SystemTime to monotonic Instant","description":"PR #360 review: execute_script_with_timeout and the on_progress callback use SystemTime/UNIX_EPOCH for deadline comparison. SystemTime can jump due to NTP or manual clock changes, causing timeouts to fire too early or never. Refactor to use std::time::Instant (monotonic) with an AtomicU64 storing elapsed nanos/millis from a baseline Instant, or store the Instant in an Arc\u003cMutex\u003e.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:49Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:49Z"}

{"id":"bd-pths","title":"Throttle Rhai timeout check to every N operations","description":"PR #360 review: The on_progress callback in rhai_engine.rs calls SystemTime::now() (or future Instant::now()) on every single operation, which is extremely frequent and adds overhead. Check the wall-clock deadline only every N operations (e.g., every 1024 or 10000 counts) to reduce per-op cost.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:52Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:29:52Z"}

{"id":"bd-sbgy","title":"Make HDF5 adaptive flush test deterministic","description":"PR #360 review: test_hdf5_writer_adaptive_flush relies on two 50ms wall-clock windows and assumes HDF5 flush completes within that time. On slow/contended CI, the sleep branch may win before the flush completes. Fix: poll batch_count() with a bounded timeout until it changes, or invoke flush_to_disk() directly after exceeding threshold.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:30:29Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:30:29Z"}

{"id":"bd-qglt","title":"Make Rhai timeout test deterministic","description":"PR #360 review: test_rhai_timeout depends on a busy-loop (1M iterations) taking \u003e50ms. On fast CI runners, it may complete before the deadline. Fix: use an unbounded loop (e.g., loop { x += 1 }) that relies solely on the timeout to terminate, with max_operations set high enough to not interfere.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:36:40Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:36:40Z"}

{"id":"bd-t8pb","title":"Rewrite Loom test to exercise real pool API","description":"PR #360 review: loom_pool_algorithm_safety models a SimpleSemaphore + SimpleQueue that doesn't reference the actual pool crate implementation. It can't validate real pool invariants. Rewrite to drive the real pool API (or model the same primitives/algorithm as the actual implementation) so the Loom verification is meaningful.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T22:36:43Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:36:43Z"}

{"id":"bd-wzdm","title":"Universal Drivers + SurrealDB + Metadata UI Convergence","description":"Converge the SCPI/TCP stack on universal TOML manifests, harden SurrealDB control-plane integration, and finish metadata-driven advanced UI composition.

Scope:
- Universal driver runtime policy and migration path
- SurrealDB metadata integrity and reconciliation behavior
- UI manifest propagation (`[ui]`) and advanced widget composition from metadata
- Legacy/deprecated path retirement and rollout guardrails
- Integration and hardware validation for lab profiles","design":"## Background
The codebase currently has a mixed state: universal factories are active in runtime registration, but legacy paths and deprecated schema/UI paths are still present. SurrealDB integration exists behind feature gates and some import paths can clobber enriched driver metadata.

## Strategy
1. Lock architecture and runtime selection policy.
2. Fix metadata integrity path first (prevent command/capability clobber).
3. Propagate universal TOML `[ui]` from raw -> validated -> runtime metadata.
4. Complete advanced UI composition over `metadata.available_commands` and UI descriptors.
5. Explicitly support lab profile selection for native/universal/hybrid+db modes.
6. Remove deprecated active-path dependencies and stabilize reconciliation/tests.

## Deliverables
- Deterministic driver runtime policy (SCPI/TCP universal-first, camera native exception)
- Metadata-complete SurrealDB records and drift reconciliation
- Advanced UI command/status/widget picker from metadata catalogs
- Integration + hardware validation and migration docs","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Architecture policy approved and documented in-repo
- [ ] No import/update path writes empty capabilities/commands unless truly absent
- [ ] Universal TOML `[ui]` is available at runtime for UI composition
- [ ] Advanced panel supports metadata-driven widget additions
- [ ] Lab launch supports explicit mode selection (native/universal/hybrid+db)
- [ ] Deprecated active UI config path removed or fully gated off
- [ ] Integration matrix and hardware validation complete
- [ ] Legacy deprecation plan published with rollback guidance","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-19T15:25:05Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-19T15:25:05Z","labels":["architecture","epic","metadata","surrealdb","ui","universal-drivers"]}

The codebase currently has a mixed state: universal factories are active in runtime registration, but legacy paths and deprecated schema/UI paths are still present. SurrealDB integration exists behind feature gates and some import paths can clobber enriched driver metadata.

{"id":"bd-8q77","title":"Isolate Device (is)","notes":"## Protocol Details (Manual p 20)

### Command
- `is` - Isolate device from group commands for N minutes

### Format
- **Request**: `A is XX` where XX = minutes in hex (00-FF)
- **Response**: `A GS 00` (status OK)

### Behavior
- Device ignores group address commands during isolation period
- Still responds to individual address commands
- Useful for maintenance/calibration without affecting other devices on bus
- `is 00` cancels isolation immediately

### Use Cases
- Isolate one device while calibrating others
- Prevent accidental group moves during maintenance
- Temporary exclusion from synchronized operations

### Implementation Notes
- Time is 8-bit value: 0x00 = cancel, 0x01-0xFF = 1-255 minutes
- Consider adding helper method: `isolate_for_minutes(n: u8)`
- May want to track isolation state locally
Reparented 2026-02-10 into Phase 5 (Product Backlog & Long-Horizon) during second cleanup pass.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-06T12:06:16Z","created_by":"briansquires","updated_at":"2026-02-10T00:43:31Z"}

{"id":"bd-izdj.25","title":"[P4] Bluesky-Compatible Metadata Export","description":"**Competitive Context**: Bluesky is the 'gold standard for metadata and reproducibility.' rust-daq should support exporting to Bluesky-compatible formats.

**Problem**: rust-daq stores data in HDF5 but doesn't follow Bluesky's metadata conventions.

**Files to modify**:
- `crates/daq-storage/src/hdf5_writer.rs`
- New: `crates/daq-storage/src/bluesky_compat.rs`
- `crates/daq-proto/proto/daq.proto` (metadata fields)

**Implementation**:
1. Research Bluesky document model:
   - RunStart, RunStop, Event, EventDescriptor, Datum, Resource
   - See: https://blueskyproject.io/event-model/
2. Add optional Bluesky-style metadata to HDF5 output:
   - `/entry/bluesky/run_start` - JSON document
   - `/entry/bluesky/descriptors/` - per-stream metadata
3. Implement `BlueskyDocumentWriter` that outputs JSON-L documents
4. Add export command: `rust-daq export --format bluesky run_123.h5`
5. Consider optional Databroker integration (MongoDB/Tiled)

**Metadata Fields to Capture**:
| Field | Source | Example |
|-------|--------|---------|
| uid | generated | "abc123..." |
| time | system clock | 1706454321.123 |
| plan_name | script name | "wavelength_scan" |
| detectors | device registry | ["prime_bsi"] |
| motors | device registry | ["maitai"] |
| scan_id | incrementing | 42 |

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] HDF5 files include Bluesky-compatible metadata group
- [ ] Export to JSON-L documents (Bluesky format)
- [ ] Can be ingested by Databroker/Tiled (verify with test)
- [ ] Documentation for Bluesky interop

**Effort**: 8-12 hours

**Strategic Value**: Allows rust-daq users to leverage Bluesky's analysis ecosystem without switching acquisition systems.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:40:40Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:40:40Z"}

{"id":"bd-izdj.26","title":"[P4] Python Bindings via PyO3","description":"**Competitive Context**: PyMoDAQ, Bluesky, ScopeFoundry all have native Python integration with NumPy/SciPy ecosystem. rust-daq lacks this.

**Problem**: Scientists want to use Python for analysis but rust-daq is Rust-only.

**Files to create** (POST-RENAME CRATE NAME):
- New crate: `crates/python/` (follows rename scheme: no daq- prefix)
- `Cargo.toml` with pyo3 + numpy dependencies
- `src/lib.rs` - Python module definition

**PyPI Package Name**: `rust-daq` (keep brand, distinguishes from crate name)

**Implementation**:
1. Create `python` crate using PyO3
2. Expose key types to Python:
   - `DaqClient` - connect to daemon, control devices
   - `Frame` - zero-copy access to frame data as NumPy array
   - `ParameterValue` - get/set device parameters
3. Use `numpy` crate for zero-copy array sharing
4. Publish to PyPI as `rust-daq` package

**Python API Design**:
```python
import rust_daq

# Connect to daemon
client = rust_daq.connect("localhost:50051")

# List devices
devices = client.list_devices()

# Read camera frame as NumPy array (zero-copy!)
frame = client.read_frame("prime_bsi")
print(frame.data.shape)  # (2048, 2048)
print(frame.data.dtype)  # uint16

# Control device
client.set_parameter("maitai", "wavelength", 800.0)
position = client.get_parameter("maitai", "wavelength")

# Subscribe to stream
for frame in client.stream_frames("prime_bsi", max_fps=30):
    # Process with NumPy/SciPy
    mean = np.mean(frame.data)
    print(f"Frame {frame.number}: mean={mean:.1f}")
```

**Zero-Copy Strategy**:
- Use `numpy::PyArray2` with shared memory backing
- Arrow arrays → NumPy via zero-copy conversion
- gRPC frames → Python with minimal copying

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] `pip install rust-daq` works
- [ ] Zero-copy frame access verified (memory doesn't double)
- [ ] All basic operations exposed (list, read, write, stream)
- [ ] Example Jupyter notebook demonstrating integration
- [ ] Performance benchmark: Python wrapper < 5% overhead

**Effort**: 12-16 hours

**Strategic Value**: Opens rust-daq to the massive Python scientific ecosystem. Scientists can use familiar tools for analysis while rust-daq handles acquisition.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:40:40Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:53:08Z"}

{"id":"bd-izdj.27","title":"[P4] Scan Plan DSL (Bluesky-Inspired)","description":"**Competitive Context**: Bluesky's 'plan' abstraction is incredibly powerful for complex, step-based scans. rust-daq's Rhai scripting is lower-level.

**Problem**: Users have to write imperative Rhai scripts. No declarative scan definition.

**Files to modify**:
- `crates/daq-scripting/src/`
- New: `crates/daq-scripting/src/plans/`

**Implementation**:
1. Create declarative scan plan DSL in Rhai:
```rhai
// Declarative 2D scan
let plan = scan_plan()
    .name("wavelength_power_map")
    .motor("maitai", "wavelength", 700..1000, 10)  // outer loop
    .motor("rotator_2", "angle", 0..360, 5)        // inner loop
    .detector("power_meter")
    .detector("prime_bsi", #{exposure: 100})
    .per_point(|| {
        sleep(50);  // settle time
    })
    .on_complete(|data| {
        save_hdf5(data, "scan_001.h5");
    });

run_plan(plan);
```

2. Implement plan executor that handles:
   - Nested loops with checkpointing
   - Automatic metadata collection
   - Pause/resume without data loss
   - Estimated time remaining

3. Built-in plan types:
   - `grid_scan(motors, detectors)` - N-dimensional grid
   - `line_scan(motor, start, stop, steps)` - 1D scan
   - `fly_scan(motor, detectors)` - continuous motion
   - `time_series(detectors, duration, interval)` - fixed-time acquisition

**Plan Metadata (Bluesky-compatible)**:
```json
{
  "plan_name": "grid_scan",
  "plan_type": "generator",
  "plan_args": {"motors": ["maitai"], "num": 30},
  "num_points": 900,
  "num_intervals": 899,
  "hints": {"dimensions": [["wavelength", "angle"]]}
}
```

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] Declarative scan builder API in Rhai
- [ ] Built-in plan types (grid, line, fly, time_series)
- [ ] Automatic checkpointing during scans
- [ ] Progress reporting with ETA
- [ ] Bluesky-compatible plan metadata

**Effort**: 12-16 hours

**Strategic Value**: Brings Bluesky's best feature to rust-daq without requiring Python.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:40:40Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:40:40Z"}

{"id":"bd-izdj.28","title":"[P4] Device Configuration Wizard (PyMoDAQ-Inspired)","description":"**Competitive Context**: PyMoDAQ's Dashboard allows drag-and-drop configuration. rust-daq requires editing TOML files.

**Problem**: Initial device setup is too technical for many scientists.

**Files to modify**:
- `crates/daq-egui/src/panels/`
- New: `crates/daq-egui/src/wizard/`

**Implementation**:
1. Create "Add Device" wizard in GUI:
   - Step 1: Select device type (Camera, Motor, Sensor, etc.)
   - Step 2: Auto-detect available ports/addresses
   - Step 3: Configure device-specific parameters
   - Step 4: Test connection
   - Step 5: Save to configuration

2. Port/device auto-detection:
   - Serial: List /dev/ttyUSB*, /dev/serial/by-id/*
   - Network: mDNS discovery for network devices
   - PVCAM: Query pl_cam_get_total()

3. Live validation:
   - Test connection during wizard
   - Show device response/ID
   - Highlight errors immediately

4. Generate TOML config:
```toml
# Auto-generated by Device Wizard
[[devices]]
id = "laser_1"
type = "maitai"
enabled = true

[devices.config]
port = "/dev/serial/by-id/usb-Silicon_Labs_..."
baud_rate = 115200
```

**Wizard UI Flow**:
```
[Select Type] → [Detect Ports] → [Configure] → [Test] → [Save]
    ↓              ↓                ↓           ↓
  Camera        /dev/ttyUSB0     Baud: 115200   ✓ OK
  Motor         /dev/ttyUSB1     Address: 2
  Sensor        (auto-detected)
```

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] Wizard accessible from GUI menu
- [ ] Auto-detection for serial ports
- [ ] Live connection test during wizard
- [ ] Generated TOML matches manual config
- [ ] Can edit existing devices via wizard
- [ ] User testing with 3 non-technical users

**Effort**: 10-14 hours

**Strategic Value**: Lowers barrier to entry. Scientists can set up rust-daq without reading TOML documentation.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:41:16Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:41:16Z"}

{"id":"bd-m4pf","title":"Clean Mechanics (cm)","notes":"## Protocol Details (Manual p 46)

### Command
- `cm` - Clean mechanics (full-range movement cycle)

### Format
- **Request**: `A cm`
- **Response**: `A GS 00` when complete

### Behavior
- Moves device over full mechanical range
- Purpose: Remove dust, debris, or particulates from mechanism
- Takes several minutes to complete
- Uses full range of motion

### Timing
- Long-running operation (several minutes)
- Motor moves throughout full range during cleaning

### Use Cases
- Periodic maintenance
- After device has been idle in dusty environment
- Before precision measurements

### Safety Considerations
- Ensure full range of motion is clear before running
- Device moves unpredictably during cleaning cycle
- Not suitable for mounted optics without clearance check

### Implementation Notes
- Consider async version with progress monitoring
- Add warning in documentation about clearance
- May need extended timeout (5+ minutes)
- Check GS status for completion/errors

{"id":"bd-3yb8.17","title":"Phase 4.2: Windows CI/CD pipeline","description":"Set up GitHub Actions CI for Windows builds and tests.

## Scope
- Windows build verification
- Mock-mode tests on Windows
- Hardware tests (manual trigger when hardware available)

## CI Configuration
```yaml
# .github/workflows/windows-libs.yml
name: LIBS Windows Build

on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'crates/driver-dover-motion/**'
      - 'crates/driver-andor-sdk3/**'
      - 'crates/driver-spirit-laser/**'
      - 'crates/driver-nidaqmx/**'
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'crates/driver-dover-motion/**'
      - 'crates/driver-andor-sdk3/**'

jobs:
  build-windows:
    runs-on: windows-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Install Rust
        uses: dtolnay/rust-action@stable
        with:
          targets: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc
      
      - name: Build LIBS drivers (mock mode)
        run: cargo build --features libs -p bin
      
      - name: Run mock tests
        run: cargo test --features libs
      
  # Manual hardware tests (self-hosted runner on LIBS machine)
  hardware-tests:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, windows, libs-machine]
    if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Run hardware smoke tests
```

{"id":"bd-izdj.13","title":"[P3] Release Build Optimization","description":"**Problem**: No LTO, default codegen-units. Missing easy performance gains.

**Root Cause**: Cargo.toml doesn't specify release optimizations.

**Files to modify**:
- `Cargo.toml` (workspace root)

**Implementation**:
Add to workspace Cargo.toml:
```toml
[profile.release]
lto = "thin"
codegen-units = 1
strip = true

[profile.release-daemon]
inherits = "release"
panic = "abort"  # Smaller binary, daemon doesn't need unwinding

[profile.release-gui]
inherits = "release"
panic = "unwind"  # GUI may benefit from panic recovery
```

**Expected Gains**:
- LTO: 3-5% runtime performance
- codegen-units=1: Better optimization, longer compile
- strip: ~30% smaller binaries
- panic=abort: ~5% smaller daemon binary

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] Release profile updated
- [ ] `cargo build --release` uses new settings
- [ ] Binary size compared before/after
- [ ] Basic benchmark compared before/after
- [ ] CI updated if needed

**Effort**: 30 minutes (quick win)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:29:46Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:29:46Z"}

{"id":"bd-izdj.14","title":"[P3] Plugin Versioning for DriverFactory","description":"**Problem**: No API version in `DriverFactory`; incompatible plugins fail silently.

**Root Cause**: Trait has no versioning mechanism. Old plugins break when trait changes.

**Files to modify**:
- `crates/daq-hardware/src/plugin/driver.rs`
- `crates/daq-hardware/src/factory.rs` (registration)
- All driver factory implementations

**Implementation**:
1. Add to DriverFactory trait:
```rust
/// Returns the plugin API version this factory was built against.
/// Current version: 1
fn api_version(&self) -> u32 { 1 }
```

2. In registry registration:
```rust
if factory.api_version() != CURRENT_API_VERSION {
    warn!("Plugin {} built for API v{}, current is v{}",
          factory.name(), factory.api_version(), CURRENT_API_VERSION);
}
```

3. Define version compatibility rules:
   - Same major version: compatible
   - Different major version: warn but attempt load
   - Add `--strict-plugin-version` flag to reject mismatches

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] `api_version()` method on DriverFactory
- [ ] Version check at registration time
- [ ] Warning logged for version mismatch
- [ ] `--strict-plugin-version` flag works
- [ ] CLAUDE.md documents versioning policy

**Effort**: 2-3 hours (quick win)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:29:46Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:29:46Z"}

{"id":"bd-rgnx.10","title":"Phase 4: Full Arrow Pipeline for Zero-Copy Storage","description":"## Objective
Leverage ArrowBatches in core.rs for zero-copy writes to Parquet/Arrow files.

## Current State
- core.rs defines ArrowBatches (first-class Arrow support)
- HDF5 is primary storage format
- Arrow conversion exists but may not be fully utilized

## Target State
- Zero-copy frame encoding to Arrow format
- Direct write to Parquet files (faster than HDF5 for time-series)
- Arrow IPC for inter-process streaming
- HDF5 as optional format, not primary

## Implementation Plan
1. Audit current Arrow usage in storage crate
2. Implement ArrowStorageWriter that writes Parquet
3. Add streaming Arrow IPC support for live analysis
4. Benchmark vs HDF5 for typical workloads
5. Make storage format configurable

## Benefits
- Zero-copy from camera to disk
- Native support in pandas, polars, DuckDB
- Faster reads for analysis pipelines
- Better compression options

## Files to Modify
- crates/storage/src/arrow_writer.rs (new/enhanced)
- crates/storage/src/lib.rs (format selection)
- crates/common/src/core.rs (ensure ArrowBatches complete)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Parquet writer implemented
- [ ] Arrow IPC streaming works
- [ ] Benchmarks show improvement over HDF5
- [ ] Configuration allows format selection

## Priority

{"id":"bd-rgnx.11","title":"Phase 4: gRPC Streaming Optimization","description":"## Objective
Optimize high-bandwidth frame streaming over gRPC.

## Current Concern
- Streaming 2048x2048 frames over gRPC using protobuf
- Serialization overhead can bottleneck CPU
- Standard protobuf may not use zero-copy

## Investigation Needed
1. Profile current gRPC frame streaming
2. Measure CPU usage during high-fps streaming
3. Check if tonic's Bytes zero-copy is utilized

## Potential Solutions
1. **Verify tonic zero-copy**: Ensure Bytes fields use zero-copy
2. **Quality modes**: Already implemented (Full/Preview/Fast)
3. **Raw TCP sidecar**: Use shared memory or TCP for video, gRPC for control
4. **Local shared memory**: For same-machine clients

## Files to Investigate
- crates/server/src/streaming.rs
- crates/protocol/src/*.proto

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CPU profiling completed
- [ ] Bottleneck identified (if any)
- [ ] Optimization implemented if needed
- [ ] Streaming handles 30fps 2048x2048 without CPU saturation

## Dependencies
- Upgrade tonic 0.10 → 0.12 first (Hyper 1.0, performance improvements)

## Priority
Lower priority - investigate before committing to optimization","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-30T16:14:19Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-30T16:14:19Z"}

{"id":"bd-rgnx.18","title":"Phase 2: HDF5 Backpressure Strategy","description":"## Objective
Define explicit behavior when HDF5 Actor channel is full.

## Rationale (from review)
This is an ESSENTIAL COMPANION to bd-rgnx.5 (HDF5 Actor). If the HDF5 writer lags behind the camera, the channel fills up. Without a defined strategy, the system will either:
- Block the camera (stalling acquisition)
- Panic (crashing the daemon)
- Silently drop data (data loss)

## Options to Evaluate
1. **Bounded Channel + Block**: Camera blocks until HDF5 catches up
   - Pro: No data loss
   - Con: Camera timing affected, potential SDK timeouts

2. **Bounded Channel + Drop Oldest**: Overwrite oldest frame in channel
   - Pro: Camera never blocks
   - Con: Data loss (but predictable)

3. **Bounded Channel + Drop Newest**: Reject new frame if channel full
   - Pro: Oldest data preserved
   - Con: Loss of most recent data

4. **Adaptive**: Reduce camera frame rate when channel pressure detected
   - Pro: Graceful degradation
   - Con: Complex implementation

## Recommended Approach
Option 2 (Drop Oldest) with metrics and logging:
- Log warning when frames dropped
- Expose metrics: frames_dropped_hdf5_backpressure
- Make channel size configurable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Backpressure strategy defined and documented
- [ ] Channel size configurable
- [ ] Metrics exposed for monitoring
- [ ] Warning logged when frames dropped
- [ ] Integration test validates behavior

## Dependencies
- bd-rgnx.5 (HDF5 Actor Pattern)","notes":"2026-02-08: Tap registry already handles frame-level backpressure. HDF5 writer uses ring buffer with existing drop policy. Remaining concern is explicit HDF5 write-queue overflow policy — deprioritized to P3.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-30T16:18:21Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-08T14:11:52Z"}

{"id":"bd-rgnx.6","title":"Phase 2: PVCAM Parameter Registration Macros","description":"## Objective
Reduce PVCAM driver boilerplate with parameter registration macros.

## Current Problem
- PvcamDriver struct (lib.rs:71-143) has repetitive parameter initialization
- create() method (lib.rs:328-470) dominated by copy-paste parameter setup
- Adding new camera feature requires changes in 4 places:
  1. Struct field
  2. Initialization
  3. Registration
  4. Connection logic
- High risk of copy-paste errors

## Implementation Plan
1. Create declarative macro for parameter definition:
```rust
pvcam_parameters! {
    exposure_time: Float {
        name: "Exposure Time",
        units: "ms",
        range: 0.1..=10000.0,
        pvcam_param: PARAM_EXPOSURE_TIME,
    },
    binning_x: Int {
        name: "Horizontal Binning",
        range: 1..=16,
        pvcam_param: PARAM_BINNING_X,
    },
    // ...
}
```

2. Macro generates:
   - Struct field definitions
   - Observable initialization
   - Registry connection
   - Getter/setter implementations

## Expected Impact
- Reduce lib.rs by ~300 lines
- Single source of truth for parameter definitions
- Easier to add new parameters
- Reduced copy-paste errors

## Files to Modify
- crates/driver-pvcam/src/lib.rs
- crates/driver-pvcam/src/macros.rs (new file)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Macro defined for parameter declaration
- [ ] At least 10 parameters migrated to macro
- [ ] Existing functionality unchanged
- [ ] Cleaner, more maintainable code","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-30T16:13:14Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-30T16:13:14Z"}

{"id":"bd-vjt0","title":"Standardize driver error types (anyhow vs DaqError)","notes":"## Problem
Driver implementations inconsistently use:
- `anyhow::Error` for internal operations
- `DaqError` for public API returns
- Some drivers wrap anyhow in DaqError, others don't

This makes error handling unpredictable for consumers.

## Current State
- daq-core defines `DaqError` enum
- Some drivers use `anyhow::Result` internally
- Public trait methods return `Result<T, DaqError>`
- Conversion happens at trait boundary

## Solution
Establish clear convention:
1. Internal driver code: Use `anyhow` for convenience
2. Public trait methods: Always return `DaqError`
3. Conversion: At trait impl boundary, map to appropriate `DaqError` variant
4. Document pattern in CONTRIBUTING.md

## Files to Audit
- crates/daq-driver-pvcam/src/**/*.rs
- crates/daq-driver-comedi/src/**/*.rs
- crates/daq-hardware/src/drivers/**/*.rs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Error handling convention documented
- [ ] All public driver methods return DaqError
- [ ] Consistent anyhow->DaqError mapping at boundaries
- [ ] No raw anyhow errors escape to consumers","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T16:58:23Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-16T16:58:23Z"}

{"id":"bd-x86h","title":"Add DAQ_PORT and DAQ_CONFIG_PATH environment variables","notes":"## Problem
Currently gRPC port and config path must be specified via CLI flags.
Environment variables would enable:
- Containerization (Docker/Kubernetes)
- systemd service configuration
- CI/CD pipeline flexibility

## Solution
Add support for environment variables:
- `DAQ_PORT` - gRPC server port (default: 50051)
- `DAQ_CONFIG_PATH` - Path to configuration file
- `DAQ_LOG_LEVEL` - Logging verbosity (optional)

## Priority Order
CLI flags should override environment variables.

## Implementation
- Parse env vars in daemon startup
- Merge with CLI args (CLI takes precedence)
- Log which source each config value came from

## Files
- crates/daq-bin/src/daemon.rs
- crates/daq-bin/src/cli.rs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] DAQ_PORT environment variable support
- [ ] DAQ_CONFIG_PATH environment variable support
- [ ] CLI flags override env vars
- [ ] Startup log shows config source
- [ ] Documentation updated","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T16:58:23Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-16T16:58:23Z"}

{"notes":"## Problem
Daemon startup currently relies on CLI flags and hardcoded defaults. Environment overrides are useful for systemd and containerized deployments.

## Updated Scope (current repo layout)
Implement optional env-var inputs in crates/bin/src/main.rs daemon command path:
- DAQ_PORT
- DAQ_CONFIG_PATH (maps to --hardware-config)
- optional DAQ_RUNTIME_MODE (must match runtime mode enum values)

## Precedence
CLI flags override env vars; env vars override built-in defaults.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Env vars parsed in daemon startup path
- [ ] Precedence behavior covered by tests
- [ ] Startup logs indicate source of resolved values
- [ ] Operations docs updated"}

{"id":"bd-3yb8","title":"EPIC: LIBS Integration into rust-daq","description":"Integrate LIBS (Laser-Induced Breakdown Spectroscopy) hardware and experiments from the Python LIBS codebase into the rust-daq unified framework.

## Overview
Port the LIBS Python codebase to rust-daq, enabling unified instrument control for both RASHG (maitai) and LIBS systems.

## Hardware Inventory (NEW - different from RASHG)
1. **Andor iStar sCMOS Camera** - Gated ICCD with DDG, MCP gain (Windows SDK)
2. **Andor ATSpectrograph** - Grating control, wavelength calibration (Windows SDK)
3. **Dover Motion XYZ Stages** - High-speed 3-axis, Trigger-On-Position (C++ API, Linux support!)
4. **Spirit Laser** - Picosecond laser via CANopen protocol
5. **NI-DAQmx** - Triggering/pulse generation (Windows)

## Key Experiments to Port
- Single-shot/multi-shot spectroscopy
- Step scans (X, Y, Z, 2D grids)
- Trigger-On-Position rapid acquisition
- Delay scans (DDG timing)
- Radiance calibration

## Architecture Notes
- Dover Motion has C++ API with Linux support (no PyO3 needed!)
- Andor SDK3 requires FFI bindings via bindgen
- Spirit laser can use existing Rust CANopen crates
- Feature flag: `libs` for LIBS-specific drivers

## Documentation
- Dover Motion API: LIBS/docs/dover-motion-api/
- Original Python code: LIBS/initialization.py, LIBS/experiments.py","design":".octocode/plan/libs-integration/plan.md","notes":"Reparented 2026-02-10 into phased backlog hierarchy (Phase 4: LIBS Integration Delivery).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-02T13:14:31Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-10T00:37:25Z","labels":["integration","libs","roadmap"]}

{"id":"bd-rgnx","title":"Codebase Modernization: Registry Refactor & Architecture Cleanup","description":"## Overview
Based on deep codebase review by external AI (Gemini 3 Pro), this epic tracks the transformation from a 'half-migrated' state to a fully modular plugin architecture.

## Current State (Problems)
1. **DriverType God Enum**: 300+ line match statement in registry.rs:1586 violates Open/Closed principle
2. **Dual Instantiation Paths**: Both factory-based (Line 1025) AND enum-based (Line 1586) coexist
3. **Conflicting Trait Definitions**: core.rs:665 vs capabilities.rs define competing Camera/Instrument traits
4. **UI Coupling in Core**: DeviceCategory::icon() puts emojis in hardware layer (capabilities.rs:138)
5. **Hardcoded Lab Config**: IP addresses and serial ports in library code (registry.rs:2350)
6. **ConfiguredDriver Enum**: factory.rs:67 defeats plugin architecture purpose

## Target State
- Purely string-based factory lookup in registry
- Single source of truth for capability traits (capabilities.rs)
- Generic UI panels generated from Parameterized metadata
- No hardcoded configuration in library code
- True plugin architecture where adding drivers requires no core changes

## Success Metrics
- Delete ~1000 lines from registry.rs
- Delete DriverType enum entirely
- UI crate size reduced by removing device-specific panels
- Adding new driver requires only: new crate + factory registration

## Phases
1. Core Architecture Cleanup (Immediate)
2. Reliability & Safety (Short Term)
3. UI Scalability (Medium Term)
4. Performance Optimization (Long Term)

## References
- Review source: Gemini 3 Pro deep analysis (2026-01-30)
- Key files: registry.rs, capabilities.rs, core.rs, factory.rs, driver.rs","notes":"2026-02-08: Gemini g3-pro deep review. Consolidated bd-rgnx.2 (trait unification) into bd-rgnx.1 (enum elimination). Closed bd-rgnx.23 (shadow mode — mocks sufficient). Core value: kill DriverType enum (bd-rgnx.1) and remove hardcoded config (bd-rgnx.8). driver-universal is complete but not integrated as primary path.
Reparented 2026-02-10 into phased backlog hierarchy (Phase 2: Architecture & Protocol Modernization).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-30T16:12:08Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-10T00:37:25Z","labels":["architecture","modernization","roadmap"]}

{"id":"bd-joh9.1.3","title":"Close timeout-to-abort gap in ScriptPlanRunner","description":"When ScriptPlanRunner times out (script-level at script_runner.rs:179 or plan-level at script_runner.rs:442-444), it returns a failure report WITHOUT calling run_engine.request_abort(). The RunEngine stays in Running state with hardware still active.

Files to modify:
- crates/scripting/src/script_runner.rs:179-188 (script-level timeout path)
- crates/scripting/src/script_runner.rs:442-444 (plan-level timeout path)
- crates/scripting/src/script_runner.rs:191-202 (max_plans limit path — also lacks abort)

Implementation (~5 lines per path):
1. In the script-level timeout block (line 179), before returning ScriptRunReport::failure, call self.run_engine.request_abort() and log a warning if it fails.
2. In the plan-level timeout (line 442-444 Err branch), before returning the anyhow error, call the same abort.
3. Audit the max_plans limit path (line 191-202) for the same gap.

Acceptance criteria:
- RunEngine transitions to Idle (via Aborting) when script times out
- No hardware operations remain in-flight after script timeout
- New test: script with long-running plan times out, verify engine state == Idle
- sg -p '$X.request_abort()' --lang rust confirms all timeout paths call abort","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-23T21:13:15Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-23T21:13:15Z","labels":["laser-safety","safety","scripting"]}

{"id":"bd-joh9.2.3","title":"Add heartbeat to run_record","description":"RunEngine updates heartbeat_at every ~10s during plan execution. Reconciler detects stale runs (heartbeat >60s old with status 'running') and marks them failed.

Files to modify:
- crates/db/src/experiment_store.rs — add update_run_heartbeat() and find_stale_runs() methods
- crates/experiment/src/run_engine.rs — spawn heartbeat task during plan execution
- crates/bin/src/reconciler.rs — detect stale runs in periodic resync

Architecture constraint: RunEngine (experiment crate) must NOT depend on db crate. Use a trait callback pattern:
  trait RunLifecycleHook: Send + Sync {
      async fn on_heartbeat(&self, run_uid: &str);
  }
The bin crate implements this with DaqDb. RunEngine accepts Option<Arc<dyn RunLifecycleHook>>.

Acceptance criteria:
- heartbeat_at updates every ~10s during plan execution
- Reconciler detects runs with stale heartbeat (>60s) and marks failed
- Test: simulate stale run, verify reconciler cleans up
- No new dependency from experiment crate to db crate","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-23T21:13:14Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-23T21:13:14Z","labels":["crash-recovery","experiment","reliability"]}

{"id":"bd-joh9.1.2","title":"Replace downcast_ref chain with rhai::serde","description":"script_value_to_dynamic() in rhai_engine.rs:480-521 uses a manual downcast_ref chain with 10+ branches. Adding new hardware types requires updating 3 parallel files. Replace with rhai::serde roundtrip for extensibility.

Files to modify:
- crates/scripting/src/rhai_engine.rs:480-521 (script_value_to_dynamic)
- crates/scripting/src/rhai_engine.rs:461-477 (dynamic_to_script_value)
- crates/scripting/src/traits.rs:76-130 (ScriptValue struct — parallel chain)

Implementation (~30 lines):
1. Keep direct conversion for primitives (i64, f64, bool, String, ()) — hot path
2. Keep explicit whitelist for opaque hardware handles (StageHandle, CameraHandle, RunEngineHandle) — not serde-compatible
3. Replace fallback with rhai::serde::to_dynamic() / rhai::serde::from_dynamic() for all other types
4. Evaluate whether to make ScriptValue implement serde traits directly

Note: Rhai 1.19 with sync feature is in use. rhai::serde module is available. Hardware handles are NOT serde-compatible, so hybrid approach needed.

Acceptance criteria:
- New hardware types only need registration in ONE place (not 3)
- All existing script tests pass
- No regression for primitive type conversion performance","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-23T21:13:14Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-23T21:13:14Z","labels":["refactor","scripting"]}

{"id":"bd-joh9.2.4","title":"Persist device runtime state","description":"Subscribe to Parameter<T> changes on registered devices and persist last-known values to SurrealDB. On restart, reconciler restores persisted state to newly registered devices. Closes gap between emergency shutdown (SafetySentinel) and graceful state restoration.

Files to modify:
- crates/db/src/config_store.rs — add upsert_device_state(), batch_upsert_device_state(), get_device_state() methods
- crates/bin/src/reconciler.rs — restore state on startup, subscribe to changes
- crates/common/src/parameter.rs — subscribe via .subscribe() (already supported, returns watch::Receiver<T>)

Implementation:
1. CRUD methods in config_store.rs for device_runtime_state table
2. After device registration in reconciler, subscribe to each Parameter<T> via .subscribe()
3. Debounce writes: collect changes for 1s, batch upsert to SurrealDB
4. On startup reconcile: read persisted state, apply to parameters via set_json()
5. Gate persistence on T: Serialize bound (not all Parameter types are serializable)

Acceptance criteria:
- Parameter changes persisted within ~1s (debounced batch)
- After simulated restart, device parameters restore to persisted values
- No hot-path impact: persist is async and debounced
- Test: set parameter → restart → verify restored","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-23T21:13:19Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-23T21:13:19Z","labels":["db","device-state","recovery"]}

**NOT in scope** (deferred): UI decomposition (app.rs 2,460 lines), blanket unwrap removal (1,427 total), full test expansion (13 untested crates), feature flag consolidation (14+ flags), SSH remote daemon management (deferred per multi-model validation — high risk, low necessity).

**Sub-epics**:
1. bd-336a — Config Foundation + GUI Presets (Phase 1-2, P1)
2. bd-1afe — Daemon Lifecycle Abstraction (Phase 3, P1) — includes Safety Sentinel, shutdown timeout, ordering fix, contract tests, tracing, audit logging

4. bd-qa36.4 — Hardware Fault Containment + Watchdog (P0)
5. bd-qa36.5 — Error Taxonomy for Hardware Control Paths (P0)
6. bd-qa36.6 — Pool Safety Audit (P0, independent) — includes Sync soundness fix, IndexGuard, max_size, loom tests, invariant docs
7. bd-qa36.7 — Test Strategy Uplift (P0)
8. bd-qa36.8 — Rhai Script Engine Security Audit (P0, independent) — Rhai sandboxing + gRPC auth
9. bd-qa36.2 — Tests + Integration Verification (Phase 6, P2)

**Dependency ordering**:
- bd-336a → bd-1afe → bd-qa36.3, bd-qa36.4, bd-qa36.5 (parallel after daemon lifecycle)

{"id":"bd-rgnx","title":"Codebase Modernization: Registry Refactor & Architecture Cleanup","description":"## Overview
Based on deep codebase review by external AI (Gemini 3 Pro), this epic tracks the transformation from a 'half-migrated' state to a fully modular plugin architecture.

## Current State (Problems)
1. **DriverType God Enum**: 300+ line match statement in registry.rs:1586 violates Open/Closed principle
2. **Dual Instantiation Paths**: Both factory-based (Line 1025) AND enum-based (Line 1586) coexist
3. **Conflicting Trait Definitions**: core.rs:665 vs capabilities.rs define competing Camera/Instrument traits
4. **UI Coupling in Core**: DeviceCategory::icon() puts emojis in hardware layer (capabilities.rs:138)
5. **Hardcoded Lab Config**: IP addresses and serial ports in library code (registry.rs:2350)
6. **ConfiguredDriver Enum**: factory.rs:67 defeats plugin architecture purpose

## Target State
- Purely string-based factory lookup in registry
- Single source of truth for capability traits (capabilities.rs)
- Generic UI panels generated from Parameterized metadata
- No hardcoded configuration in library code
- True plugin architecture where adding drivers requires no core changes

## Success Metrics
- Delete ~1000 lines from registry.rs
- Delete DriverType enum entirely
- UI crate size reduced by removing device-specific panels
- Adding new driver requires only: new crate + factory registration

## Phases
1. Core Architecture Cleanup (Immediate)
2. Reliability & Safety (Short Term)
3. UI Scalability (Medium Term)
4. Performance Optimization (Long Term)

## References
- Review source: Gemini 3 Pro deep analysis (2026-01-30)

{"id":"bd-izdj.13","title":"[P3] Release Build Optimization","description":"**Problem**: No LTO, default codegen-units. Missing easy performance gains.

**Root Cause**: Cargo.toml doesn't specify release optimizations.

**Files to modify**:
- `Cargo.toml` (workspace root)

**Implementation**:
Add to workspace Cargo.toml:
```toml
[profile.release]
lto = "thin"
codegen-units = 1
strip = true

[profile.release-daemon]
inherits = "release"
panic = "abort"  # Smaller binary, daemon doesn't need unwinding

[profile.release-gui]
inherits = "release"
panic = "unwind"  # GUI may benefit from panic recovery
```

**Expected Gains**:
- LTO: 3-5% runtime performance
- codegen-units=1: Better optimization, longer compile
- strip: ~30% smaller binaries
- panic=abort: ~5% smaller daemon binary

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] Release profile updated
- [ ] `cargo build --release` uses new settings
- [ ] Binary size compared before/after
- [ ] Basic benchmark compared before/after
- [ ] CI updated if needed

**Effort**: 30 minutes (quick win)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:29:46Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-19T20:18:29Z","defer_until":"2026-06-01T00:00:00Z"}

{"id":"bd-izdj.14","title":"[P3] Plugin Versioning for DriverFactory","description":"**Problem**: No API version in `DriverFactory`; incompatible plugins fail silently.

**Root Cause**: Trait has no versioning mechanism. Old plugins break when trait changes.

**Files to modify**:
- `crates/daq-hardware/src/plugin/driver.rs`
- `crates/daq-hardware/src/factory.rs` (registration)
- All driver factory implementations

**Implementation**:
1. Add to DriverFactory trait:
```rust
/// Returns the plugin API version this factory was built against.
/// Current version: 1
fn api_version(&self) -> u32 { 1 }
```

2. In registry registration:
```rust
if factory.api_version() != CURRENT_API_VERSION {
    warn!("Plugin {} built for API v{}, current is v{}",
          factory.name(), factory.api_version(), CURRENT_API_VERSION);
}
```

3. Define version compatibility rules:
   - Same major version: compatible
   - Different major version: warn but attempt load
   - Add `--strict-plugin-version` flag to reject mismatches

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] `api_version()` method on DriverFactory
- [ ] Version check at registration time
- [ ] Warning logged for version mismatch
- [ ] `--strict-plugin-version` flag works
- [ ] CLAUDE.md documents versioning policy

**Effort**: 2-3 hours (quick win)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:29:46Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-19T20:18:29Z","defer_until":"2026-06-01T00:00:00Z"}

{"id":"bd-izdj.25","title":"[P4] Bluesky-Compatible Metadata Export","description":"**Competitive Context**: Bluesky is the 'gold standard for metadata and reproducibility.' rust-daq should support exporting to Bluesky-compatible formats.

**Problem**: rust-daq stores data in HDF5 but doesn't follow Bluesky's metadata conventions.

**Files to modify**:
- `crates/daq-storage/src/hdf5_writer.rs`
- New: `crates/daq-storage/src/bluesky_compat.rs`
- `crates/daq-proto/proto/daq.proto` (metadata fields)

**Implementation**:
1. Research Bluesky document model:
   - RunStart, RunStop, Event, EventDescriptor, Datum, Resource
   - See: https://blueskyproject.io/event-model/
2. Add optional Bluesky-style metadata to HDF5 output:
   - `/entry/bluesky/run_start` - JSON document
   - `/entry/bluesky/descriptors/` - per-stream metadata
3. Implement `BlueskyDocumentWriter` that outputs JSON-L documents
4. Add export command: `rust-daq export --format bluesky run_123.h5`
5. Consider optional Databroker integration (MongoDB/Tiled)

**Metadata Fields to Capture**:
| Field | Source | Example |
|-------|--------|---------|
| uid | generated | "abc123..." |
| time | system clock | 1706454321.123 |
| plan_name | script name | "wavelength_scan" |
| detectors | device registry | ["prime_bsi"] |
| motors | device registry | ["maitai"] |
| scan_id | incrementing | 42 |

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] HDF5 files include Bluesky-compatible metadata group
- [ ] Export to JSON-L documents (Bluesky format)
- [ ] Can be ingested by Databroker/Tiled (verify with test)
- [ ] Documentation for Bluesky interop

**Effort**: 8-12 hours

**Strategic Value**: Allows rust-daq users to leverage Bluesky's analysis ecosystem without switching acquisition systems.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:40:40Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-19T19:55:28Z","defer_until":"2026-07-01T00:00:00Z"}

{"id":"bd-izdj.26","title":"[P4] Python Bindings via PyO3","description":"**Competitive Context**: PyMoDAQ, Bluesky, ScopeFoundry all have native Python integration with NumPy/SciPy ecosystem. rust-daq lacks this.

**Problem**: Scientists want to use Python for analysis but rust-daq is Rust-only.

**Files to create** (POST-RENAME CRATE NAME):
- New crate: `crates/python/` (follows rename scheme: no daq- prefix)
- `Cargo.toml` with pyo3 + numpy dependencies
- `src/lib.rs` - Python module definition

**PyPI Package Name**: `rust-daq` (keep brand, distinguishes from crate name)

**Implementation**:
1. Create `python` crate using PyO3
2. Expose key types to Python:
   - `DaqClient` - connect to daemon, control devices
   - `Frame` - zero-copy access to frame data as NumPy array
   - `ParameterValue` - get/set device parameters
3. Use `numpy` crate for zero-copy array sharing
4. Publish to PyPI as `rust-daq` package

**Python API Design**:
```python
import rust_daq

# Connect to daemon
client = rust_daq.connect("localhost:50051")

# List devices
devices = client.list_devices()

# Read camera frame as NumPy array (zero-copy!)
frame = client.read_frame("prime_bsi")
print(frame.data.shape)  # (2048, 2048)
print(frame.data.dtype)  # uint16

# Control device
client.set_parameter("maitai", "wavelength", 800.0)
position = client.get_parameter("maitai", "wavelength")

# Subscribe to stream
for frame in client.stream_frames("prime_bsi", max_fps=30):
    # Process with NumPy/SciPy
    mean = np.mean(frame.data)
    print(f"Frame {frame.number}: mean={mean:.1f}")
```

**Zero-Copy Strategy**:
- Use `numpy::PyArray2` with shared memory backing
- Arrow arrays → NumPy via zero-copy conversion
- gRPC frames → Python with minimal copying

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] `pip install rust-daq` works
- [ ] Zero-copy frame access verified (memory doesn't double)
- [ ] All basic operations exposed (list, read, write, stream)
- [ ] Example Jupyter notebook demonstrating integration
- [ ] Performance benchmark: Python wrapper < 5% overhead

**Effort**: 12-16 hours

**Strategic Value**: Opens rust-daq to the massive Python scientific ecosystem. Scientists can use familiar tools for analysis while rust-daq handles acquisition.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:40:40Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-19T19:55:28Z","defer_until":"2026-07-01T00:00:00Z"}

{"id":"bd-izdj.27","title":"[P4] Scan Plan DSL (Bluesky-Inspired)","description":"**Competitive Context**: Bluesky's 'plan' abstraction is incredibly powerful for complex, step-based scans. rust-daq's Rhai scripting is lower-level.

**Problem**: Users have to write imperative Rhai scripts. No declarative scan definition.

**Files to modify**:
- `crates/daq-scripting/src/`
- New: `crates/daq-scripting/src/plans/`

**Implementation**:
1. Create declarative scan plan DSL in Rhai:
```rhai
// Declarative 2D scan
let plan = scan_plan()
    .name("wavelength_power_map")
    .motor("maitai", "wavelength", 700..1000, 10)  // outer loop
    .motor("rotator_2", "angle", 0..360, 5)        // inner loop
    .detector("power_meter")
    .detector("prime_bsi", #{exposure: 100})
    .per_point(|| {
        sleep(50);  // settle time
    })
    .on_complete(|data| {
        save_hdf5(data, "scan_001.h5");
    });

run_plan(plan);
```

2. Implement plan executor that handles:
   - Nested loops with checkpointing
   - Automatic metadata collection
   - Pause/resume without data loss
   - Estimated time remaining

3. Built-in plan types:
   - `grid_scan(motors, detectors)` - N-dimensional grid
   - `line_scan(motor, start, stop, steps)` - 1D scan
   - `fly_scan(motor, detectors)` - continuous motion
   - `time_series(detectors, duration, interval)` - fixed-time acquisition

**Plan Metadata (Bluesky-compatible)**:
```json
{
  "plan_name": "grid_scan",
  "plan_type": "generator",
  "plan_args": {"motors": ["maitai"], "num": 30},
  "num_points": 900,
  "num_intervals": 899,
  "hints": {"dimensions": [["wavelength", "angle"]]}
}
```

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] Declarative scan builder API in Rhai
- [ ] Built-in plan types (grid, line, fly, time_series)
- [ ] Automatic checkpointing during scans
- [ ] Progress reporting with ETA
- [ ] Bluesky-compatible plan metadata

**Effort**: 12-16 hours

**Strategic Value**: Brings Bluesky's best feature to rust-daq without requiring Python.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:40:40Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-19T19:55:28Z","defer_until":"2026-07-01T00:00:00Z"}

{"id":"bd-rgnx.4","title":"Phase 2: Add Frame Pool Timeout Protection","description":"## Objective
Prevent UI stalls from crashing hardware by adding timeouts to frame pool acquisition.

## Current Problem
- FrameProducer uses pool::Loaned<FrameData> for zero-copy streaming
- If UI holds 100% of pool buffers (paused debugger, memory leak, slow processing):
  - Camera driver blocks indefinitely waiting for buffer
  - Hardware loop hangs
  - Potential SDK timeout/crash
  - No visibility into the issue

## Implementation Plan
1. Add timeout parameter to pool buffer acquisition
2. If pool empty for >1 second, drop the frame instead of blocking
3. Log warning: "Frame Dropped (Pool Empty) - consumers too slow"
4. Add metrics: pool_exhaustion_count, frames_dropped_backpressure
5. Consider adaptive behavior: reduce frame rate when pool pressure detected

## Code Location
- crates/pool/src/lib.rs (buffer acquisition)
- crates/driver-pvcam/src/acquisition.rs (frame producer loop)
- crates/driver-mock/src/mock_camera.rs (reference implementation)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Timeout on buffer acquisition (configurable, default 1s)
- [ ] Frame dropped instead of blocking when pool exhausted
- [ ] Warning logged with context
- [ ] Metrics exposed for monitoring
- [ ] Camera driver never blocks indefinitely

## Testing
- Create test that holds all pool buffers
- Verify driver continues running (dropping frames)
- Verify warning logged","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-30T16:13:13Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-01-30T16:13:13Z"}

2. **Quality modes**: Already implemented (Full/Preview/Fast)
3. **Raw TCP sidecar**: Use shared memory or TCP for video, gRPC for control

{"id":"bd-rgnx.18","title":"Phase 2: HDF5 Backpressure Strategy","description":"## Objective
Define explicit behavior when HDF5 Actor channel is full.

## Rationale (from review)
This is an ESSENTIAL COMPANION to bd-rgnx.5 (HDF5 Actor). If the HDF5 writer lags behind the camera, the channel fills up. Without a defined strategy, the system will either:
- Block the camera (stalling acquisition)
- Panic (crashing the daemon)
- Silently drop data (data loss)

## Options to Evaluate
1. **Bounded Channel + Block**: Camera blocks until HDF5 catches up
   - Pro: No data loss
   - Con: Camera timing affected, potential SDK timeouts

2. **Bounded Channel + Drop Oldest**: Overwrite oldest frame in channel
   - Pro: Camera never blocks
   - Con: Data loss (but predictable)

3. **Bounded Channel + Drop Newest**: Reject new frame if channel full
   - Pro: Oldest data preserved
   - Con: Loss of most recent data

4. **Adaptive**: Reduce camera frame rate when channel pressure detected
   - Pro: Graceful degradation
   - Con: Complex implementation

## Recommended Approach
Option 2 (Drop Oldest) with metrics and logging:
- Log warning when frames dropped
- Expose metrics: frames_dropped_hdf5_backpressure
- Make channel size configurable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Backpressure strategy defined and documented
- [ ] Channel size configurable
- [ ] Metrics exposed for monitoring
- [ ] Warning logged when frames dropped
- [ ] Integration test validates behavior

## Dependencies
- bd-rgnx.5 (HDF5 Actor Pattern)","notes":"2026-02-08: Tap registry already handles frame-level backpressure. HDF5 writer uses ring buffer with existing drop policy. Remaining concern is explicit HDF5 write-queue overflow policy — deprioritized to P3.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-30T16:18:21Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-19T20:18:32Z","defer_until":"2026-06-01T00:00:00Z"}

{"id":"bd-9n9k.3","title":"SurrealDB readiness guardrails: explicit startup failures/warnings and engine visibility in UI/health","description":"Harden startup and health UX around SurrealDB readiness so operators can clearly see active engine/path and failure mode.","design":"Add explicit fail/warn policies by mode, include engine/path in health endpoints and relevant UI surfaces, and improve operator-facing messaging for missing engine features or path errors.","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Startup emits actionable failure/warning guidance by mode\n- [ ] Active DB engine/path visible in health and UI\n- [ ] Misconfiguration scenarios covered by tests","notes":"CODEBASE RESEARCH (2026-02-20):
- DB init at daemon_manager.rs:238-267 — non-fatal (prints warning, continues without DB)
- DaqDb::info() at core.rs:133-155 returns DbInfo with engine/path/health/counts
- GetDbInfo gRPC RPC already exists at config_service.rs:328-343 (proto daq.proto:2698-2707)
- GAPS:
  1. custom_health_service.rs does NOT include DB health in system health responses
  2. Startup only prints eprintln warning on failure — no structured tracing::warn! with actionable guidance
  3. No runtime-mode-aware fail policy (e.g., hybrid-db mode should FAIL if DB init fails, not silently degrade)
  4. UI has ZERO DB visibility — needs panel or status bar showing engine/path/health
- EXISTING: ConfigService.GetDbInfo works end-to-end, good unit+integration test coverage for DB core
- FILES TO MODIFY:
  - daemon_manager.rs: mode-aware startup fail/warn policy
  - custom_health_service.rs: add DB health to system health
  - crates/ui/: add DB status display (call GetDbInfo)","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-19T19:15:13Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-24T19:38:30Z","labels":["gate:sunset","observability","surrealdb","ui"]}

Completed protocol/backend spectrum streaming workstream with additive StreamSpectra RPC, client support, UI spectrum plot channel/panel support, decimation/ring-buffering, and compatibility/migration design documentation.

Implemented LiveVisualizationPanel spectrum decimation and ring-buffering (bounded spectrum channel, capped snapshot history, display-point decimation) with unit tests for endpoint preservation and buffer limits.

{"notes":"HARDENING EXECUTION PLAN

Research completed 2026-02-28. The UI crate (`crates/ui/`) contains ~3500 lines in app.rs alone, with 85+ `#[cfg(` annotations across the crate. The codebase already has solid WASM stub infrastructure (panels/mod.rs wasm_stubs, runtime.rs abstraction, time.rs abstraction) but several hardening gaps remain.

## Key Findings

1. **Native-only deps in general `[dependencies]`**: `experiment`, `hardware`, `clap`, `which`, `dirs`, `tracing-subscriber` are NOT behind `cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))` in Cargo.toml. They live in the shared dependency section but are only used by native-only modules. This is the highest-risk item: if any of these crates pull in sys-deps that fail WASM compilation, the web build breaks.

2. **Unguarded `std::process::Command` in shared code**: `app.rs:2976` calls `std::process::Command::new("rerun").spawn()` inside `render_nav()` which is called on both platforms. This will fail or panic on WASM.

3. **Constructor duplication**: `DaqApp::new()` (native, ~300 lines) and `DaqApp::new_wasm()` (WASM, ~130 lines) share ~60% identical initialization logic (theme, shortcuts, style, dock state, device panels, orphan cleanup). This duplication means any bugfix to initialization must be applied twice.

4. **Config-driven panels native-only**: `docked_config_driven_panels`, `config_cache`, `grpc_ui_config_cache` are `#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))]` fields. On WASM, config-driven device panels silently do nothing rather than showing a stub message.

5. **Module structure divergence**: `lib.rs` gates modules by `target_arch` while `main.rs` gates by `feature = "standalone"`. The web feature path (`lib.rs` -> `web_main.rs`) uses a different module gating pattern. This double-gating is correct but fragile.

6. **WASM connection has dead code path**: `wasm_connect()` at app.rs:2711 sets up `connect_rx` channel infrastructure (lines 3173-3191 in update()) but `wasm_connect()` itself does synchronous connection (lines 2721-2726), making the channel polling dead code.

7. **gui_config.rs filesystem access**: `find_gui_config_path()` uses `std::env::current_exe()` and filesystem operations that fail on WASM. The function itself is only called from native paths, but it has no explicit cfg gate.

## Slice 1: Cargo.toml Dependency Isolation
**Scope**: `crates/ui/Cargo.toml`
**Description**: Move `experiment`, `hardware`, `clap`, `which`, `dirs`, `tracing-subscriber` from the shared `[dependencies]` section into `[target.'cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))'.dependencies]`. These crates are only used behind `#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))]` modules. Currently they compile (and link) for WASM even though they are dead code on that target. This is the cheapest fix with the largest blast radius reduction.
**Rollback**: Single-file revert: `git checkout HEAD -- crates/ui/Cargo.toml`
**Verification**:
  - `cargo check -p ui` (native default features)
  - `cargo check -p ui --features web --target wasm32-unknown-unknown` (WASM web build)
  - `cargo nextest run -p ui` (all unit tests)

## Slice 2: Platform-guard Shared UI Code (render_nav + gui_config)
**Scope**: `crates/ui/src/app.rs` (render_nav method), `crates/ui/src/gui_config.rs`
**Description**: Add `#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))]` guard around the "Open Rerun" button that calls `std::process::Command::new("rerun").spawn()` at app.rs:2972-2977. On WASM, either hide the button or show a disabled placeholder. Also add explicit cfg gate to `gui_config.rs::find_gui_config_path()` to prevent accidental future calls from WASM paths. These are surgical, bounded fixes to shared code that does not touch constructors or the update loop.
**Rollback**: `git checkout HEAD -- crates/ui/src/app.rs crates/ui/src/gui_config.rs`
**Verification**:
  - `cargo check -p ui` (native)
  - `cargo check -p ui --features web --target wasm32-unknown-unknown` (WASM)
  - `cargo nextest run -p ui` (unit tests)
  - Manual: confirm native GUI still shows Rerun button and it works

## Slice 3: Deduplicate Constructor Initialization (new vs new_wasm)
**Scope**: `crates/ui/src/app.rs` (DaqApp::new, DaqApp::new_wasm, plus new helper methods)
**Description**: Extract shared initialization logic from `DaqApp::new()` (lines ~1155-1452) and `DaqApp::new_wasm()` (lines ~1457-1617) into a private helper method (e.g., `DaqApp::init_common(cc, runtime, app_settings, shortcut_manager, theme_preference, control_panel_layout_mode) -> CommonInit`). The helper would handle: font setup, theme loading, shortcut loading, style config, health channel creation, device panel restoration, dock state init, and orphan cleanup. Each platform constructor would call the helper and then set platform-specific fields. This reduces the two ~60%-overlapping constructors to a single source of truth for shared init.
**Rollback**: `git checkout HEAD -- crates/ui/src/app.rs`
**Verification**:
  - `cargo check -p ui` (native)
  - `cargo check -p ui --features web --target wasm32-unknown-unknown` (WASM)
  - `cargo nextest run -p ui` (all unit tests including app tests)
  - Manual: confirm both native and WASM apps start correctly with persisted state

## Slice 4: Clean Up WASM Connection Dead Code
**Scope**: `crates/ui/src/app.rs` (WasmConnectionState, wasm_connect method, update polling)
**Description**: The `WasmConnectionState.connect_rx` field and the async polling block in `update()` (lines 3173-3191) are dead code because `wasm_connect()` performs a synchronous connection and never populates `connect_rx`. Either: (a) remove the dead channel and simplify `WasmConnectionState`, or (b) convert `wasm_connect()` to actually use the async channel pattern for proper error handling. Option (b) is preferred because it would enable connection timeout and retry UI for WASM users. This is a WASM-only change that does not touch native code paths.
**Rollback**: `git checkout HEAD -- crates/ui/src/app.rs`
**Verification**:
  - `cargo check -p ui --features web --target wasm32-unknown-unknown`
  - `cargo check -p ui` (native should be unaffected)
  - Manual: test WASM connection to a running daemon in browser

## Slice 5: WASM Config-Driven Panel Stubs
**Scope**: `crates/ui/src/panels/mod.rs` (wasm_stubs module), `crates/ui/src/app.rs` (render_device_control)
**Description**: On WASM, config-driven panels (`docked_config_driven_panels`, `config_cache`, `grpc_ui_config_cache`) are completely absent, causing TOML-manifest devices to show as generic panels with no UI schema. Add a WASM stub that parses the `ui_schema_json` from the gRPC DeviceInfo (already available on both platforms) and renders a basic read-only control panel using the existing `web_schema.rs` types. This improves WASM parity without touching native config loading.
**Rollback**: `git checkout HEAD -- crates/ui/src/panels/mod.rs crates/ui/src/app.rs`
**Verification**:
  - `cargo check -p ui --features web --target wasm32-unknown-unknown`
  - `cargo nextest run -p ui` (web_schema deserialization tests)
  - Manual: WASM build shows device schema controls for TOML-manifest devices

## Execution Order

Slices are ordered by risk-to-value ratio (safest first):
1. **Slice 1** (Cargo.toml) -- zero code changes, just dep restructuring
2. **Slice 2** (render_nav guard) -- 2 files, surgical, prevents runtime panics
3. **Slice 4** (WASM dead code) -- WASM-only, no native impact
4. **Slice 3** (constructor dedup) -- app.rs refactor, highest code churn but bounded to constructors
5. **Slice 5** (config-driven stubs) -- new feature, lowest priority hardening"}

Prevent broad cross-file surgery in single PRs.","acceptance_criteria":"1) At least 3 concrete bounded slices are documented as sub-steps in notes with file scope and owner. 2) No single slice touches both platform entrypoints and core app state orchestration without explicit justification. 3) Each slice has a rollback note and verification commands recorded.","notes":"Purpose: reduce merge risk and isolate regressions during post-merge hardening.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T19:45:26Z","created_by":"TheFermiSea","updated_at":"2026-02-28T21:14:56Z"}
